topic: Quantified result — dome author's own claim taxonomy applied to the 69 confirmed WINs
exp_reference: EXP-170 — Timestamp Error tab April 2026 update (already contains the per-WIN tag enumeration; this file adds the aggregate count)
executive_summary: Of the 69 WINs the dome author displays as "confirmed," only 6 unique visible-label WINs have been explicitly classified in the public claim_index.json under his newly published methodology.json taxonomy. Of those 6, ZERO are classified as prospective_confirmed — the only class of the five that, by the author's own definition, counts as a genuine forecast. The remaining 63 WINs are unclassified, i.e., the author has not yet committed to a taxonomy label for them in the public API. The single prospective_confirmed label in the published index is on pred-eclipse-tier3, a pending prediction (status prospective, not confirmed). Under the author's own taxonomy, applied to the data he has himself published, the prospective_confirmed column of a taxonomy-aware scoreboard currently contains zero confirmed WINs.
kernel_of_truth: methodology.json is a real methodological improvement. Distinguishing retrospective structural arguments from genuine pre-registered forecasts is standard scientific practice, and the author publishing this distinction is progress. The claim_index.json classifications he has made are defensible on their face — win-069 and win-062 really are retrospective structural derivations; win-058-bedrock really is pending independent verification; win-068-ensemble really is not uniquely dome-only. Credit for making the distinction publicly is earned. The problem is not the taxonomy. The problem is that applying the taxonomy collapses the headline.

per_classified_WIN.title: Solar Elevation Formula Emerges from H(r)

self_selection_observation: The six classified WINs are a non-random sample. They include the author's four strongest_current_targets (WIN-069, WIN-062, PRED-ECLIPSE-TIER3, WIN-068), plus the two critical_open_problems explicitly enumerated in methodology.json (WIN-058-bedrock, WIN-013 — though WIN-013 is described but not tagged in claim_index.json). The author has classified the items he is most willing to defend in public. The 63 unclassified WINs have not yet been forced into the taxonomy on the public surface — a conservative reading is that classification is ongoing, but a less charitable reading is that tagging each of the remaining 63 would produce more non-prospective labels and further erode the "69 confirmed" tagline. Either way, the current public record, scored under the author's own rules, does not support the headline.
relation_to_review_text: EXP-170 (Timestamp Error tab April 2026 update) already enumerates each tagged WIN individually in paragraph 3 of its replacement_html. This supplementary finding adds the aggregate result (6-of-69 tagged, 0-of-6 prospective_confirmed, 63 unclassified) for use by the decider if a concise one-line kill-shot formulation is wanted anywhere on the site (e.g., killshots tab headline block, predictions tab header). The aggregate result is consistent with and does not contradict EXP-170's per-WIN enumeration.
counterpoint: The author may not yet have finished classifying the 69 WINs; claim_index.json is dated 2026-04-17 (same day as publication). The 63 unclassified WINs may be labelled in the coming days, and some may eventually carry prospective_confirmed — particularly the V13/V51 solar-geometry predictions whose parameters were published before the confirming data was pulled.

The claim_index.json file is explicitly a partial sample — registry_integrity_notes focuses on the WIN-058/WIN-062 collision disambiguation. The strongest_current_targets subset in methodology.json is not claimed to be exhaustive. A fair reading is "these are the items the author chose to highlight," not "these are the only items the author considers classifiable."

Even if zero of the classified 6 are prospective_confirmed today, that does not mean no WIN in the full 69 can ever earn that label. The taxonomy itself is a structural improvement and we should monitor for the author adding prospective_confirmed entries later (which would be the structural canary fix we planted, only in a weaker form).

recommended_actions.action: Optionally cite the aggregate count (6-of-69 tagged, 0-of-6 prospective_confirmed under confirmed status) in the Kill-Shots tab header or Predictions tab lead paragraph
recommended_actions.rationale: EXP-170 already covers the per-WIN enumeration in the Timestamp Error tab. Citing the aggregate elsewhere compresses the argument into a single sentence a non-specialist can follow.
recommended_actions.proposed_sentence: As of 2026-04-17 the dome author has begun classifying his claims under his own five-class taxonomy; only six of the sixty-nine "confirmed" WINs have been tagged so far, and not one of those six sits in the prospective_confirmed class he himself reserves for genuine forecasts.

recommended_actions.action: Re-run this count against claim_index.json on 2026-04-24 to track whether prospective_confirmed tags appear among the 63 currently-unclassified WINs
recommended_actions.rationale: If the author continues classifying, the empty prospective_confirmed column is either filled (structural partial fix) or stays empty (reinforcing the kill-shot). Either outcome is useful.

recommended_actions.action: No modification to EXP-170 recommended — it is complete and the per-WIN enumeration there is more readable than the aggregate
recommended_actions.rationale: Avoid resetting the curmudgeon review cycle for a cosmetic addition.

gotchas: Do not report "0 of 69 prospective_confirmed" as a bare headline — it is 0 of 6 classified (63 unclassified), which is a materially weaker statement. Stick to the accurate compound claim.

Do not assume the pattern will hold as the remaining 63 are classified. Some solar-geometry WINs (V13 / V51 predictions) may legitimately earn prospective_confirmed if the author can show parameter registration predated the confirming data pull. Monitor and adjust.
Our own review also uses multiple classes of evidence; we should not set a standard the author meets when we do not meet it ourselves. Caveat in any use of this finding.
newsworthy: Dome author publishes a five-class claim taxonomy; his own partial classification of his registry shows zero of the six classified "confirmed" WINs land in the prospective_confirmed column his taxonomy defines as genuine pre-registered forecasts.
